title: Dog Crusty Cracked Paw Pads: Causes and Care Guide
meta_desc: Learn about causes for dog crusty paw pads, why the urgency level is low, and how triage photos help your vet provide the best care for your pet.
content_html:
TL;DR: Crusty or cracked paw pads are usually caused by an overproduction of keratin and are rarely an emergency unless you see bleeding, limping, or signs of infection. Scheduling a routine vet visit and providing a clear photo can help determine the underlying cause and treatment.

What causes my dog's paw pads to look crusty, cracked, or thickened?

It can be distressing to see your furry friend's paws looking dry, thickened, or jagged. This condition, often known as paw pad hyperkeratosis, occurs when your dog's body produces too much keratin. Common causes include:

  • Paw pad hyperkeratosis resulting in a "hairy" appearance.
  • Environmental irritants.
  • Seasonal allergies.
  • Specific nutritional deficiencies.

Are crusty paw pads a medical emergency for my dog?

While crusty paw pads can look uncomfortable, they are rarely a medical emergency. If your dog is walking normally and not showing signs of extreme pain, you can typically wait for a routine appointment. However, seek immediate veterinary advice if you notice:

  • Deep bleeding.
  • A foul odor suggesting a potential infection.
  • Sudden limping or inability to put weight on the paw.

How does sending a photo of my dog's paws help with veterinary triage?

When you contact a professional for advice, a clear, close-up photograph is one of the most valuable tools you can provide. A high-quality photo helps professionals because:

  • Different conditions, such as zinc-responsive dermatosis or autoimmune issues, can look very similar to simple dryness.
  • It allows a vet to see the specific pattern of crusting and the depth of any cracks.
  • It helps determine the best course of action and urgency before you step into the clinic.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Familial footpad hyperkeratosis, reported in Irish Terriers and Dogue de Bordeaux, involves all pads of all feet from a young age. Severe hyperkeratosis leads to horns, fissures, secondary infections, pain, and lameness. Treatment is symptomatic, including soaking, keratolytic and emollient treatments, and management of bacterial pyoderma. Major differential diagnoses for footpad hyperkeratosis include hepatocutaneous syndrome.

Chapter: Dermatology, General Principles

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1116)

faqs.question: Can I use human moisturizer on my dog's paws?
faqs.answer: It is best to avoid human lotions as they may contain chemicals or scents that are toxic if licked. Always use a dog-specific paw balm or plain coconut oil.

faqs.question: Is paw pad hyperkeratosis painful?
faqs.answer: The thickened skin itself is not usually painful, but if the crusts become thick enough to crack, it can cause discomfort similar to a callous or a blister for your dog.

faqs.question: How can I prevent my dog's paws from cracking?
faqs.answer: Regular use of dog-safe balms and avoiding hot pavement or harsh ice-melt chemicals can help maintain healthy paw pad hydration.
